prompt:
We got burned Tuesday: a deploy ran half its schema changes, died on step 3, and the next deploy happily re-ran steps 1 and 2 on top of themselves. I want a proper migration runner as a library we embed in our services. Module go-migrunner, package migrunner, stdlib only, no database driver — migrations are plain Go funcs the caller provides.

API:
- Migration{ID string; Up func() error; Down func() error}. Down == nil means irreversible.
- NewRunner(statePath string, migrations []Migration) (*Runner, error). Migrations run in slice order. Reject empty ids, duplicate ids, nil Up. The runner keeps its applied history in a file at statePath (format is your call; no file yet = fresh install). On load, the applied history must be exactly a prefix of the configured migration order — if the file mentions a migration we no longer have, or the order doesn't line up, refuse to construct rather than guess.
- Plan() []string — pending ids in run order, a pure dry run, never executes anything. Applied() []string — applied ids in order.
- Up() ([]string, error) — run every pending migration in order. Record each success before moving on, so a failure mid-sequence leaves the state file exactly reflecting what actually ran: earlier successes stay applied, the failing one and everything after stay pending, and the error names the migration that failed. Re-running Up after a fix must resume at the failed step, never re-run the ones that succeeded. Up with nothing pending is a no-op.
- Down(n int) ([]string, error) — roll back the last n applied migrations in reverse order, recording each step as it goes; same consistency rule if a Down func fails (error names the culprit, state matches what ran). n < 1 or n > applied count is an error that runs nothing. If any migration in the span has a nil Down, refuse up front without rolling anything back.

runner_test.go and rollback_test.go are the acceptance contract — don't edit them; go test -race -timeout 30s ./... must pass. Layout: I want the state-file persistence separated from the run/rollback orchestration (separate files), because the state format will grow fields later.
